ranges.label: Low Fear of Negative Evaluation
ranges.description: Your fear of negative evaluation is low, indicating that you are generally comfortable in social situations and not overly preoccupied with how others judge you. This suggests healthy self-confidence and social functioning.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your healthy perspective on social evaluation and interpersonal interactions
Your confidence in social situations is a strength that supports both personal and professional relationships
Stay mindful that occasional social concern is normal, and your balanced approach serves you well

ranges.label: Moderate Fear of Negative Evaluation
ranges.description: You have a moderate level of concern about being negatively evaluated by others, which may cause discomfort in some social situations. This degree of social worry is common but can escalate without active management strategies.
suggestions: Practice self-compassion when you notice yourself worrying about what others think of you
Challenge negative automatic thoughts about social evaluation by examining the evidence for and against them
Consider whether your concerns are proportionate to the actual social situation you are facing
Gradually expose yourself to mildly uncomfortable social situations to build confidence over time
Develop a brief grounding technique you can use when social anxiety arises in the moment

ranges.label: High Fear of Negative Evaluation
ranges.description: You have a high level of fear of negative evaluation, which is likely interfering with your social functioning and daily activities. This pattern is a core feature of social anxiety disorder and responds well to structured treatment.
suggestions: Consider consulting a mental health professional who specializes in social anxiety for a comprehensive assessment
Cognitive behavioral therapy has strong evidence for reducing fear of negative evaluation and improving social confidence
Practice gradual exposure to feared social situations with the support of a therapist or trusted companion
Learn to identify and challenge cognitive distortions such as mind-reading and catastrophizing in social contexts

ranges.label: Very High Fear of Negative Evaluation
ranges.description: Your fear of negative evaluation is very high and is likely significantly impacting your daily life, work, and social interactions. At this severity level, professional treatment is strongly recommended, as effective interventions exist.
suggestions: Seeking professional help from a therapist experienced in social anxiety is strongly recommended at this level
Social anxiety is one of the most treatable anxiety disorders, with high response rates to evidence-based therapies
Discuss both psychotherapy and medication options with a qualified mental health professional
Remember that avoidance of social situations, while providing short-term relief, maintains and worsens fear over time
Consider group therapy for social anxiety, which provides both treatment and safe social practice opportunities

ranges.label: Normal Range
ranges.description: Your child’s total score is within the normal range, indicating no significant psychosocial problems across the domains assessed. Children scoring in this range are generally functioning well emotionally, behaviorally, and socially.
suggestions: Continue monitoring your child’s emotional and behavioral health through regular open conversations
Maintain consistent routines and age-appropriate expectations to support ongoing healthy development
Stay engaged with your child’s school and peer activities to reinforce positive social functioning

ranges.label: Clinically Significant
ranges.description: Your child’s total score has reached a clinically significant level, suggesting meaningful psychosocial difficulties that warrant further professional evaluation. Review the subscale scores to identify specific areas of concern.
suggestions: Consult a child mental health professional for a comprehensive evaluation to identify specific problem areas
Review the subscale scores to determine whether internalizing, attention, or externalizing problems are most prominent
Communicate with teachers and school counselors about your child’s behavior across different settings
Maintain a supportive and nonjudgmental home environment while seeking appropriate professional guidance
Consider completing the PSC-17 again after three months to track changes following any interventions

internalizing.label: Normal
internalizing.description: Your child’s internalizing score is within the normal range, suggesting no significant emotional difficulties such as sadness, worry, or hopelessness. Children in this range are generally coping well with their emotional experiences.

internalizing.label: Elevated
internalizing.description: Your child’s internalizing score is elevated, indicating possible emotional distress such as persistent sadness, excessive worry, or feelings of hopelessness. Further evaluation by a mental health professional is recommended.

attention.label: Normal
attention.description: Your child’s attention score is within the normal range, indicating that concentration, focus, and activity levels are generally age-appropriate. No significant attention-related difficulties are suggested by this score.

attention.label: Elevated
attention.description: Your child’s attention score is elevated, suggesting difficulties with concentration, restlessness, or distractibility that may be affecting school performance. Further evaluation for attention-related problems is recommended.

externalizing.label: Normal
externalizing.description: Your child’s externalizing score is within the normal range, suggesting that behavioral conduct, peer interactions, and rule-following are generally appropriate for their age. No significant behavioral concerns are indicated.

externalizing.label: Elevated
externalizing.description: Your child’s externalizing score is elevated, indicating possible behavioral problems such as fighting, defiance, or difficulty understanding the feelings of others. Professional assessment can help identify targeted behavioral interventions.

ranges.label: Secure Attachment
ranges.description: Your scores suggest a relatively secure attachment style characterized by low avoidance and low anxiety in close relationships. You are comfortable with intimacy and interdependence, and do not excessively worry about abandonment.
suggestions: Continue nurturing your close relationships through open communication, trust, and mutual support
Your relational patterns appear healthy and adaptive, serving as a strong foundation for emotional well-being
Be aware that attachment security can shift during major life transitions, so maintain your supportive relationship habits

ranges.label: Moderate Attachment Concerns
ranges.description: Your scores suggest some degree of attachment insecurity that may manifest as worry about relationships or discomfort with closeness. Review your subscale scores to determine whether anxiety or avoidance is more prominent.
suggestions: Reflect on which relationships feel most secure and identify what specific qualities make them feel safe
Practice open and honest communication with trusted individuals about your needs and concerns
Notice patterns of withdrawal or clinginess in your relationships and gently challenge those automatic responses
Consider reading about attachment theory to better understand your relational tendencies and their origins

ranges.label: Significant Attachment Insecurity
ranges.description: Your scores suggest notable attachment insecurity that is likely affecting your relationship satisfaction and emotional well-being. High scores may reflect deep fears of abandonment, strong discomfort with intimacy, or both.
suggestions: Consider speaking with a therapist who specializes in attachment and relationship issues for professional guidance
Explore attachment-focused therapy approaches such as Emotionally Focused Therapy or schema therapy
Practice identifying your emotional needs in relationships without judgment or self-criticism
Build awareness of how early relationship experiences may be shaping your current attachment patterns
Develop a self-soothing toolkit for moments when relationship anxiety or avoidance becomes overwhelming

avoidance.label: Low Avoidance
avoidance.description: You are comfortable with emotional closeness and depending on others in your relationships. You can openly share your feelings, seek support when needed, and trust that others will be responsive to you.

avoidance.label: Moderate Avoidance
avoidance.description: You sometimes find it difficult to open up emotionally or depend on others, preferring self-reliance in stressful situations. This pattern may limit the depth and satisfaction of your close relationships over time.

avoidance.label: High Avoidance
avoidance.description: You tend to strongly avoid emotional closeness, self-disclosure, and depending on others in relationships. This defensive pattern often stems from early experiences and can prevent you from forming deeply satisfying bonds.

anxiety.label: Low Anxiety
anxiety.description: You feel relatively secure in your relationships and do not worry excessively about abandonment or rejection. You can tolerate temporary separations and trust that important people in your life care about you.

anxiety.label: Moderate Anxiety
anxiety.description: You sometimes worry about being abandoned, unloved, or not valued enough by important people in your life. These concerns may lead to reassurance-seeking behaviors that can strain your relationships over time.

anxiety.label: High Anxiety
anxiety.description: You frequently worry about rejection, abandonment, and whether others truly care about you in your close relationships. This intense preoccupation can lead to emotional volatility and difficulty trusting partners.

ranges.label: At Risk for Social Isolation
ranges.description: Your total score suggests you may be at risk for social isolation, with limited social networks in both family and friendship domains. Social isolation is a significant health risk factor associated with increased morbidity and mortality.
suggestions: Consider reaching out to family members or friends you have lost touch with to rebuild social connections
Look for community activities, clubs, religious organizations, or volunteer opportunities that align with your interests
Talk to a healthcare provider about local resources and programs designed to promote social engagement
Start with small, manageable social interactions and gradually increase their frequency and depth over time
Consider using technology such as video calls or social platforms to maintain connections with distant family and friends

ranges.label: Moderate Social Network
ranges.description: You have a moderate level of social engagement with some meaningful connections, but there may be room to strengthen your social network. Investing in both family and friend relationships can provide broader support and resilience.
suggestions: Continue nurturing your existing relationships through regular contact and meaningful interactions
Consider expanding your social network by joining new groups or activities that interest you
Maintain a balance between family and friend connections, as both serve different supportive functions
Schedule regular social activities to ensure consistent engagement rather than relying on spontaneous contact
Deepen existing relationships by sharing more personal matters and offering reciprocal support

ranges.label: Strong Social Network
ranges.description: You have a strong social network with good connections to both family and friends, providing robust social support. Strong social networks are protective against depression, cognitive decline, and many physical health conditions.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your strong social connections through regular meaningful interactions with family and friends
Your social network is a valuable resource for well-being, resilience, and health across the lifespan
Consider supporting others in your community who may be experiencing social isolation or loneliness
Stay open to forming new connections while maintaining the depth and quality of your existing relationships

family.label: At Risk
family.description: Your family network is limited, with few relatives you see regularly, confide in, or can call upon for help. Strengthening family connections or building alternative support systems can improve your overall social well-being.

family.label: Moderate
family.description: You have a moderate family network with some relatives you maintain contact with and feel comfortable turning to. There may be opportunities to deepen these connections or reconnect with other family members.

family.label: Strong
family.description: You have a strong and supportive family network, with multiple relatives you see regularly, confide in, and can rely on for help. This provides an important foundation for emotional support and practical assistance.

friends.label: At Risk
friends.description: Your friend network is limited, with few friends you see regularly, confide in, or can call upon for help. Building friendships through shared activities or community involvement can significantly enhance your social support.

friends.label: Moderate
friends.description: You have a moderate friend network with some friends you maintain contact with and feel comfortable turning to. Investing time in these friendships and seeking new social opportunities can strengthen this network.

friends.label: Strong
friends.description: You have a strong and supportive friend network, with multiple friends you see regularly, confide in, and can rely on for help. Friendships provide unique social support that complements family relationships.
